coredata.dc:title: Formation of titanium hydride in Ti–6Al–4V alloy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Liangshun
Su, Yanqing
Guo, Jingjie
Fu, Hengzhi
coredata.dc:description: Use of hydrogen as a temporary element in titanium alloys is an attractive approach for enhancing processability, and also for modification of microstructures and thereby improving final mechanical properties. In this article, microstructures, phases and phase transformations in Ti–6Al–4V alloy specimens containing 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5wt.% hydrogen has been investigated using optical microscopy, XRD and TEM. It was shown that there are fcc δ titanium hydride plates precipitated in the specimens containing hydrogen 0.302 and 0.490wt.%. Simultaneously, mass orthorhombic martensite α″ has been found in the specimens as well as δ hydride. One eutectoid transformation mechanism from βH to δ hydride and α based on diffusion transformation theory was presented.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium alloys
Hydride
Eutectoid transformation

article.ce:title: Formation of titanium hydride in Ti–6Al–4V alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Use of hydrogen as a temporary element in titanium alloys is an attractive approach for enhancing processability, and also for modification of microstructures and thereby improving final mechanical properties. In this article, microstructures, phases and phase transformations in Ti–6Al–4V alloy specimens containing 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5wt.% hydrogen has been investigated using optical microscopy, XRD and TEM. It was shown that there are fcc δ titanium hydride plates precipitated in the specimens containing hydrogen 0.302 and 0.490wt.%. Simultaneously, mass orthorhombic martensite α″ has been found in the specimens as well as δ hydride. One eutectoid transformation mechanism from βH to δ hydride and α based on diffusion transformation theory was presented.

ce:section.ce:para: Duplex titanium alloys containing α+β phases are widely used in the aerospace, marine, nuclear and many other industries. But the high costs of machining attribute to the low limit of deformation, high deformation resistance, high flow stress, low strain rate and high hot working temperature are the bottleneck of the large scale application of duplex titanium alloys. In recent years, thermohydrogen processing (THP) has proved to be an effective way to modify the microstructures and enhance the mechanical properties and processability [1–13]. Titanium hydride is sure to play an important part in the modification and enhancement [4–9]. However, there are still some conflicts about the formation of titanium hydride in duplex titanium alloys [14–21]. Xiao [15] used the model of martensitic phase transformation to explain the mechanism of the hydride formation in titanium alloys. However, he did not consider the diffusion of hydrogen [10,16].

In the present work, the effects of hydrogen on the microstructures, phases and phase transformations in Ti–6Al–4V alloy containing 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5wt.% hydrogen has been investigated. And the formation of titanium hydride plates was discussed in detail.

The material used was α+β type Ti–6Al–4V alloy bar 6mm in diameter. The chemical composition of the material is shown in Table 1 . The bars were hydrogenated by holding at 850°C in a pure hydrogen atmosphere for 1.5h, following a procedure described in Fig. 1 . The hydrogenation procedure is described elsewhere [11]. The hydrogen content of the specimens were controlled by hydrogenation time, the partial pressure of hydrogen and last determined by weighing the specimens before and after hydrogenation treatment. These measurements were accurate to 10−5 g.

All metallographic specimens were ground and polished by conventional techniques and etched with Kroll's reagent (10%HF:10%HNO3:80%H2O). Phase analysis was performed using XRD (D/max-rb, Cu Kα, 40kV) and TEM (Philips CM-12, 120kV). Thin foil specimens for TEM observations were prepared using twin-jet electropolishing apparatus with a solution of methanol, 1-butanol and perchloric acid (60:34:6 volume ratio). Hydrogen evolution in the thin foils was avoided by keeping the temperature of the electrolytic bath between −35 and −40°C.

Microstructures of as-received and hydrogenated Ti–6Al–4V alloy with different hydrogen content are shown in Fig. 2 . After hydrogenation, α lamellar structure appears in the specimen with 0.102wt.%H (Fig. 2b), and lots of α″ martensite plates appear in the specimens with 0.302wt.%H and 0.490wt.%H (Fig. 2c and d). The X-ray diffraction patterns of the phases present before and after hydrogenation are shown in Fig. 3 . The as-received and with 0.102wt.%H alloy show a simple X-ray diffraction pattern of α (hcp) phase and β (bcc) phase (Fig. 3a and b). In the samples with hydrogen content of 0.302 and 0.490wt.%, an orthorhombic martensite phase α″ is detected (Fig. 3c and d). A characteristic face-centered cubic (fcc, a =0.444nm) hydride δ appears in the specimen with 0.490wt.%H (Fig. 3d).

More detailed microstructural information is given by TEM. Figs. 4 and 5
show the photomicrograph of δ hydride and eutectoid α lamellae neighboring to each other. Most of the eutectoid transformation products (δ+α) growing from primary α (α0) grain boundaries proves that the eutectoid transformation mostly taking place along the primary α grain boundaries. Fig. 6 shows the orthorhombic martensite α″ plates with fine twins in the specimens with 0.302wt.%H and 0.490wt.%H.

The effects of hydrogen on the phase transformations in titanium alloys have received many investigations in detail since 1980s. But the formation of δ hydride in titanium alloys is still in dispute [9–21]. Xiao [15] used the model of martensitic phase transformation to explain the mechanism of the hydride formation in titanium alloys, without considering the diffusion of hydrogen [16]. However, the diffusion of hydrogen cannot be ignored because of its considerable diffusivity D during the experiment, which can be described by the following equations [10,16]:
(1)
D
α
= 1.8 ×
10
− 6
exp
−
6230
T
(2)
D
β
= 1.95 ×
10
− 7
exp
−
3342
T
where D α is diffusivity of hydrogen in α phase, D β is diffusivity of hydrogen in β phase and T is the temperature.

Besides the diffusion of hydrogen, the cooing rate also plays an important part in the model of eutectoid transformation [15]. As a result, there must be one critical cooling rate V d, below which the diffusion of hydrogen plays the dominant part in the eutectoid transformation and above which it is the shear mechanism. In the present work (Fig. 1), the eutectoid transformation is based on the diffusion of hydrogen because (1) the diffusion of hydrogen has enough time because of the small cooling rate (cooling in furnace); (2) the diffusivities D α, D β are considerable because of the high temperature (650–850°C) and (3) the shear mechanism of βH →α+δ is so difficult that the c-axis of β must expand for 40%. βH is the phase of β containing hydrogen.

According to Section 3.1 above, one eutectoid transformation mechanism from βH to δ hydride and α based on diffusion transformation theory was presented. After hydrogenation, most of α translated into βH (Fig. 7a) according to the Ti–6Al–4V–H pseudo phase diagram [14], as is shown in Fig. 8 . And the reaction β→α happens when cooling in furnace to room temperature, Fig. 1. Most of the primary α precipitates along the grain boundaries in βH phase (Fig. 7b and c). The eutectoid reaction βH (bcc)→α (hcp)+δ (fcc) happens (Fig. 7d) when temperature reduce to the eutectoid temperature. Because the solubility of hydrogen in α phase is very small (≤7.9at%) [8], so one zone rich in hydrogen emerges around the primary α (α0), as is shown in Fig. 9 . The richness of hydrogen in the zone can promote the precipitation of δ hydride, which has a fcc structure of CaF2 type in which hydrogen atoms randomly occupy tetrahedral [8–21]. Most of the δ hydride precipitates along the primary α grain boundary because of the high density of defects. The δ hydride lamellae grow via the supply of hydrogen atoms from βH and primary α. Most of hydrogen atoms come from βH because the hydrogen concentration in primary α phase is very small. The growth of δ hydride will create one zone lean in hydrogen which can promote the formation of eutectoid α phase. The zone around δ hydride turns into eutectoid α when the hydrogen concentration in which below the critical concentration in α phase. This is the coupled growth of δ hydride and eutectoid α. After the eutectoid transformation, one eutectoid zone in which δ hydride and eutectoid α neighboring to each other emerges, as is shown in Figs. 4, 5 and 7d and e.

It is noticed that the eutectoid transformation is relatively slow and most of the eutectoid transformation takes place along the grain boundaries because of the clustering of hydrogen atoms along the grain boundaries [9]. A considerable decrease of the critical cooling rates [14] led to the formation of martensitic structure α″ phase in the hydrogenated specimens during cooling in air to room temperature. This case can be explained by the stress induced by the supersaturation of hydrogen during the cooling in air to room temperature [6–8]. The martensitic transformation βH (bcc)→α″ is very quick and the products are main martensite plates, as is shown in Figs. 6 and 7f.
The microstructures of hydrogenated Ti–6Al–4V alloy were studied. It was shown that δ titanium hydride plates precipitates in the specimens containing 0.302wt.%H and 0.490wt.%H.

Orthorhombic structure martensite α″ phase exists in the specimens as well as δ hydride plates. It was estimated that there is one critical cooling rate V d, above which the diffusion of hydrogen plays the dominant part in the eutectoid transformation and below which it is the shear mechanism. One eutectoid transformation mechanism from βH with low hydrogen concentration (<0.5wt.%) to δ hydride and α based on diffusion transformation theory was presented.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to Beijing Aeronautical Manufacturing Technology Research Institute of China for preparation of the hydrogenated Ti–6Al–4V alloy and the authors gratefully acknowledge the support of NSFC project No. 50271020 and 50395102.
